title: Cultivate Critical Thinking
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Cultivate Critical Thinking.  
This comes from Leviticus 10:10-11. You must distinguish between what is sacred and what is 
common, between what is ceremonially unclean and what is clean. And you must teach the 
Israelites all the decrees that the Lord has given them through Moses.

When we say critical thinking, we don't mean negativity, like finding out what's bad about 
someone. What it means to think, what is the critical issue here? What is the important, 
essential or central issue? It's about cultivating discernment, about being, ask, ask the 
question that you're able to distinguish between contrasting points of view and be able 
understand and go, well, this and that aren't the same. And you can't have both of these 
things, or they are the same being able to think through thoughtfully and evaluate and discern.

title: Guard Your Heart
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Guard Your Heart.    
Proverbs 4:20-23.  My child pay attention to what I say, listen carefully to my words. Don’t lose 
sight of them. Let them penetrate deep into your heart... Love that. Let them penetrate deep 
into your heart. ... for they bring life to those who find them and healing to the whole body. 
Guard your heart above all else, for it determines the course of your life.

Friends, we have to watch what goes into our heart and what comes out of our heart. When 
you program computers, they say GIGO -garbage in garbage out. If you're feeding your mind 
and your heart all the time with garbage, garbage is going to come out. Let's, let's discipline 
our minds to take in that which is godly and righteous and beneficial, in every area to learn of 
the ways of God in creation, in science and mathematics and literature and whatever area to 
learn of his ways. And to allow his excellence to then be rooted in our hearts, His character, His 
ways.

title: Love Good and Hate Evil
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Love Good and Hate Evil.  
Proverbs 9:10. This fear of the Lord is the foundation of wisdom. Knowledge of the Holy One 
results in good judgment.

This echoes what we see in Proverbs 8:13 that all who fear the Lord will hate evil. So education 
- we’re being trained to discern to have critical thinking. So it's not just that we can assess, but 
we can devote ourselves to the good and run away, shun, avoid the evil. That is, that is what 
God has called us to do.

title: Faith ... Virtue ... Knowledge …
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Faith. Virtue. Knowledge.  
This comes from 2 Peter 1:5-8. For this very reason, make every effort to add to your faith, 
goodness, or virtue or character, depending on your translation and to goodness, virtue of 
character knowledge and to knowledge self-control and to self-control perseverance and to 
perseverance godliness, and to godliness mutual affection and to mutual affection. Love. For if 
you possess these qualities in increasing measure... Look at this promise. If you possess these 
qualities of increasing measure, they will keep you from being ineffective and unproductive in 
your knowledge of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Do you want to be fruitful? Do you want to be effective? You want to be productive? Then grow 
in faith, in virtue, in knowledge, and self-control, in perseverance, and godliness, affection, and 
love. 

This order is significant. To your faith add virtue. And then knowledge. Sometimes when we 
think of education the goal is to get to know more, is we focus on the cognitive head stuff. But 
it says to faith first, add virtue, goodness, moral character. We need to be shaped, in our hearts 
June 2023Page  of 2736

and then trained in our mind and develop skills for our hands. When we do those together, then 
we will be effective and productive.

This passage is foundational for the University of the Nations, YWAM's university I'm involved 
with. And that's why the way we get into this university is not by passing an entrance exam 
that tests your knowledge. You do a Discipleship Training School that develops your character. 
And that character development is more important than the mere acquisition of knowledge.

True education is discipleship. It's about bringing in transformation in the whole person. 
Sometimes education is kind of like a watermelon on a stick. The head gets big and round and 
the rest of the body and the rest of your life is undeveloped and scrawny like a little stick-man, 
a little pole-man, but with as big watermelon size head. That's not God's vision. That we are all 
in an integrated holistic way developed and head, heart, hands - all who we are - grows 
together in the purposes of God.
reflection_q1: Why does building character through virtue matter as much as acquiring knowledge?
reflection_q2: How should this progression of qualities shape educational objectives and methods?

title: Role of the Family in Education
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
The Role of The Family in Education.   
Proverbs 3:1-6.  My child never forget the things I've taught you. Store my commands in your 
heart. If you do this, you will live many years and your life will be satisfying. Never let loyalty 
and kindness leave you! Tie them about your neck as a reminder. Write them deep within your 
heart. Then you'll, then you'll find favor with both God and people, and you will earn a good 
reputation. Trust in the Lord with all your heart; do not depend on your own understanding. 
Seek his will in all you do, and he will show you which path to take.

This passage as myriads of other passages throughout scripture, some of which we've already 
looked at, we see that family is the foundational sphere for which education occurs. Parents 
are enjoined to teach their children. Children are exhorted to learn from their parents. Family is 
God's and God's design is the primary place for training the next generation.

title: Role of the Church in Education
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Role of the Church in Education.  
This is Acts 19:9-10.  He took the disciples with him and had discussions daily in the lecture 
halls of Tyrannus. This is Paul when he's in Ephesus on his second missionary journey. This 
went on for two years, so that all the Jews and Greeks who lived in the province of Asia heard 
the word of the Lord.

Remember we just said that the family is the foundation for education? Well, the church is the 
family of God. So by extension through implicational, critical thinking, we can realize that as the 
family of God, they also have a key role to intentionally participate in the formation of new 
members who are born into the fellowship of believers. So this is a role that's shared and built 
upon what family does.

title: Role of the School in Education
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Role of the School and Education.   
1 Chronicles 27:32 is talking about some of the people who worked in various occupations in 
David's palace when he was King.  Jonathan, David's uncle was a wise counselor to the king, a 
man of great insight and a scribe. Jehiel the Hacmonite was responsible for teaching the king's 
son.

The tutor, the teacher. We're to celebrate the many people who are called to the vocation of 
teaching. It's a high calling. Because it's so important for our children to grow up in the ways of 
God, to grow with understanding, discover their gifts, understand how God's designed them 
and what the intents and purposes He has for their life, to develop that legacy  —we just need, 
we need many other people supporting the family, supporting the church in that process. And 
so that God calls many to be teachers and we need to see a revival of this at this time. This is a 
June 2023Page  of 2836

high calling. It's a spiritual calling. It's a significant calling. When you, when you are teaching 
the next generation preschool, elementary, or high school, you making a difference, not just for 
today, but for years to come.

title: Support Good Teachers\!
category_title: Principles and Practices of Biblical Education
transcript:
Support Good Teachers.  
This comes from 3 John 1 there is only one chapter in 1John. Verses:6-8. ...Please send them 
on the way in a manner that honors God. It was for the sake of the name that they went out 
receiving no help from the pagans. We ought therefore to show hospitality to such people so 
that we may work together for the truth.

Who is he talking about? Traveling teachers, missionaries who are preaching and teaching the 
gospel and who are informing people about the character and ways of God. And so the context 
of this exhortation, in fact, this little book could be called, entitled, open the door, you know, to 
get hospitality, to traveling teachers.

And that's in contrast to 2 John, which is the letter just before that you could entitle “close the 
door” because there are some bad teachers, some false teachers that were traveling and say, 
you know, don't even feed them a meal, don’t let them stay in your house, don’t give them any 
money. Because if you do, you participate in that. But when there are good teachers give them 
hospitality, support them financially. This was the exhortation in the early church.

For today, for our application, as we need to support financially, those who are involved with us 
in teaching our children, who are using their teaching gifts to advance good in our 
communities. And it's a meritorious task and it deserves to be supported in every way you can 
with encouragement, with prayers, and with finances.

SECTION INTRODUCTION 
Now the next six passages are going to have key elements of Biblical Education. And the 
language might sound unusual to you if you're not a teacher, but if you're a trained educator, 
you're go like, huh, I get it! Because these next passages talk about Bloom's Taxonomy. What 
is that? Well, you can look it up on Google and get all kinds of information, but it's an 
educational framework that helps people understand the process of development, of how we 
grow in our knowledge in the educational process. This taxonomy was developed in the 20th 
century by Mr. Bloom and colleagues. It rings true because it reflects principles that we find in 
scripture and that we experience in the reality of day-to-day educational experiences.

One of the things that's important in this process of discipling the nations, we have to sphere 
appropriate language to bring discipleship and transformation, biblical transformation into that 
language. So we don't use religious language in the educational sphere. We use educational 
language. So if this is seems unusual to you, well, it's not, if you're an educator, so join us on 
this journey. And I hope this will be inspirational to you.

See, Bloom's taxonomy talks about six different areas. It talks about remembering, 
understanding, applying, analyzing, evaluating, and creating. It’s usually pictured it as a 
pyramid. And the remembering is at the foundation, is the basics. And as you grow, you are 
able to master a subject matter until you can create into something totally new with that. All six 
of these steps in the educational process are abundantly evidenced in the scripture. So we'll 
take a look at some of them and see how applicable the word of God is to this sphere.

reflection_q2: What actions can communities take to support and encourage teachers who are committed to biblical education?

title: Remembering
transcript:
is Remembering.  
Deuteronomy 4:10, never forget the day - Remember - when you stood before the Lord your 
God at Mount Sinai, where he told me, Summon the people before me, and I will personally 
June 2023Page  of 2936

instruct them. Then they will learn to fear me as long as they live and they will teach their 
children to fear me also.

So Remembering has to do with recalling basic facts and concepts. You have to know the 
data. If you don't have that, you can't grow in your knowledge. So yes, you do have to 
memorize important dates in your country's history, the big events. Those are important things 
to do. You do need to know facts and data. Remembering God's work in our past, equips us to 
fear Him in the present and prepares us to teach our children in the future. Remembering has 
past, present and future implications. So its really important.

title: Understanding
transcript:
Understanding.  
Haggai 2:18 says, think about this 18th day of December, the day when the foundation of the 
Lord's temple was laid. Think carefully.

Think carefully. Haggai is a fun prophet. He's a, just a handful of short prophecies and every 
single one of them, he dates. He's the only prophet who does this. He knows exactly the 
moment when God said this thing, when this particular thing happened. So he's remembering 
the big thing that happened. So Haggai lives at the same time as Zechariah, and Nehemiah, 
and Ezra, so those four books all kind of go together. And the big thing was that the temple 
began to be rebuilt on this day. And so you need to understand what God was doing. That's 
what think carefully, understand the Bible, being able to explain ideas or concepts. This is why 
this was going on. This was why it is significant. This is why it is important. So think carefully 
Ponder on your experience and gain insight. That's that's an important part. You got to know 
the facts, but then you have to work the facts and gain, understanding.

title: Applying
transcript:
(Applying) 
For our 43rd key passage Applying. 1 Corinthians 14:35. If they want to inquire about 
something, they should ask their own husbands at home.

Oh my goodness. This is a talking about women were speaking in church and it sounds like a 
slap in the face until you get the context. In that day, women were not allowed education. There 
was no educational opportunity, not for Roman women, not for Greek women, not for Jewish 
women. In fact, rarely did men and women even worship together. In all of the pagan religions, 
they're usually just religions for men or religions for women and the religion that had men and 
women together usually had a lot of sexual kind of activities going on. Not exactly what we 
wanted to see happen in the Church. 

So as they were coming together, this is the first time this was a religious experience where 
men and women are worshiping God, the true God in a new way. There was no reference point. 
Even among the Jewish people, in the synagogues, the men worshiped in one part of the 
building and the women were relegated to either the back or the balcony, and were not part of 
the worship. They were observers from a distance. So this was something totally new in that 
era. 

Because the women had never had an opportunity for education, things could arise in the 
church. They were illiterate. They couldn't read so many of these things. They would not 
understand something. So some of them like those question asking, going on a lot of 
confusion and just not knowing how to do the process right.

And so Paul says, if they want to inquire about something, that's a good thing. They want to 
understand what this is about. And then it says they should ask their own husbands at home. 
What is significant here is this the first time in human history, by implication, that husbands are 
enjoined to make sure their wives have the same educational opportunity that they have. This 
is the first time there's an instruction in any literature of any philosophy of any religion in that 
June 2023Page  of 3036

part of the world that says, you are to invest time in educating a female. And that is a 
remarkable thing because husbands now have responsibility, they had to apply this. There was, 
there was something to be done. They were to talk about it here and we get some, they didn't 
have the full understanding. Remember where we go from the memory to understanding that 
they had set up a teaching situation in an appropriate place to continue to grow and cultivate 
this ability. This is the first opportunity. And this leaves applying always about using information 
in new situations. Wow, this was a new situation. Women had never been offered the equal 
opportunity in education until the gospel of Jesus came.

title: Analyzing
transcript:
Analyzing  
Job 42:7. After the Lord had finished speaking to Job, he said to Eliphaz the Temanite: I'm 
angry with you and your two friends, for you have not spoken accurately about me, as my 
servant Job has. 

Analysis is to look at the difference between those things that are different from one another or 
can see those things that are similar to one another. Drawing conclusions, contrasting and 
comparing ideas. This is what God is doing in, in speaking to Eliphaz in this passage of Job. 
And God's a thoughtful God. He's a thinking God and he invites us to be imitators of him in this 
process. 

We see this not only in the Book of Job, but very from the very beginning in Genesis one. Every 
day that God creates, he stands back at the end of the day, looks at what he has made, he 
analyzes it, he gives it nomenclature. He calls the light day in the darkness night, and then he 
considers what he's done and he says, it is good. It is good. There's an evaluation, a reflection, 
a pausing, a learning. And that that is something very powerful. And that's in the scripture to 
model for us. When we are in the process of growth, we create something new, we stand back, 
we reflect, we pause, we consider, we analyze and we draw conclusions. 

Remember when we are looking at the process of education, the apex of Bloom's taxonomy 
that we're going through at this point is creativity. And so this analysis is an essential part for 
creativity to happen. That's why this passage in Genesis one is so important. 
Number 45. Evaluating.  
We are reading Numbers 36:1-12. Now, I am just reading a summary of this. It's about an 
unusual situation that happened. This, this passage is actually recorded in five different 
passages. This situation that we're going to read about just keeps coming up again and again, 
because the law is that the inheritance goes through the male in the family line in, in the Jewish 
culture. But there was this one man, Zelophehad,  who only had daughters. And so since he 
had no sons, it meant that there was no one to pass on the land, the inheritance, and that 
family, that whole clan would lose their property rights. And so they had to say, well, this is the 
law, but this is an unusual circumstance. What do we do here? And so they make an 
adjustment and so they start applying that adjustment. 

And then there's another problem because if the ladies, the daughters marry outside of the 
tribe, then the land and in next generation becomes, when there is a man there, it goes to 
another tribe. So now we've made an exception, but we need to qualify that exception. 

So you see this whole story, that first emerges in Number 26, then in Numbers 27, gets 
revisited in Numbers 36 which we will read here, then picks up again in Joshua 17 and 1 
Chronicles 7. And this story keeps developing because they are evaluating. They're trying to 
figure out how to make all these principles mesh together in a way that no injustice is done and 
that good is established for all. You have to work the truth.

June 2023Page  of 3136

So let me read this. Then the heads of the clans of Gilead - descendants of Makir, son of 
Manasseh, son of Joseph -  came to Moses and then family leaders of Israel with a petition...  
daughters as Zelophehad had did as the Lord commanded Moses macula, Tirzah Ola Milka 
and Noah, all married cousins on their father's side. They married into the clans of Manasseh, 
son of Joseph. Thus their inheritances of land remained within their ancestral tribe.

So they carry the inheritance, but they married within the tribe. And so the next generation, 
everyone was happy.

To evaluate is to justify a stand or a posture or a decision. I'm taking this stand. And this is why, 
and you can explain it. You can validate it, that's what is going on here. And they're working 
this out as a new nation, trying to figure out how they're going to do this. And it's a wonderful 
example of really a multi-year, even a multi-generational process of trying to understand the 
ways of God and applying them in a way that is just and fair and kind to everyone in that 
society. We were all invited to do the same.

title: Creating
transcript:
(Create) 
The last stage, the educational process. If you've rightly remembered and understood and 
applied and analyzed and evaluated that now you are ready to Create.  Psalm 98:1 says, Sing a 
new song to the Lord, for he has done wonderful deeds. His right hand has won a mighty 
victory; His holy arm has shown is saving power!

Sing a new song, produce a new or original work. That's creativity. Do something that's never 
been done before! A new song is one of many things that could be done. And they are writing a 
new song, because they have fresh insight into who God is. He's done a new work in their 
midst. They're reflecting on that. They remember the facts. They've understood why he's doing 
it. They've evaluated. They've done all of these things and now we get it. And we're able to 
write a new song and everyone can participate. That's the culmination of this learning process, 
in this situation. It's a release of creativity.

True education doesn't confine people and put them into boxes. It doesn't, it doesn't churn out 
students like you turn out cars on an assembly line. It doesn't equip you to be ticky- tacky, 
matching little people-ettes. It allows people to develop their full potential and become the 
creative beings made in the image of God that they were intended to be.

We see this in the story of Revelation. They sing new songs even in heaven. It means that 
there's going to be education going on in heaven. If we're singing new songs in heaven, people 
are going to be writing those songs. And we'll be learning those songs, we'll be practicing 
those songs. It's exciting.

I remember as an eight or nine year old, as I was growing up as the son of missionaries in the 
high Andes mountains of Bolivia in South America, I had a Sunday school teacher who would 
often speak about the book of Revelation. She would get really excited about it. And I wasn't 
so sure. So one of her favorite passages, she talked about Revelation 4:6-11, and how they 
were worshiping God in heaven and talks about these 24 elders and these four living creatures 
and - it was kind of like spooky to me, because these living creatures had all these wings and 
their eyes all over the wings on the inside and outside. And she explained that meant they saw 
a lot. They understood a lot. Of all the beings, they were the most intelligent beings in all the 
universe. And then it says in verse eight that they sing a song, holy, holy, holy is the Lord our 
God who was and is, and as it is to come. And they did that day and night forever and ever. 

Now I was eight and I thought, these are the most intelligent beings in the universe and they 
have this one song with just a few words that they sing over and over again, day and night for 
ever and ever. That sounds really boring. And I was quite determined that I did not want to 
participate in such activity. Fortunately God changed my heart years on. And I found that there 
was great reason, but I still didn't understand why this what's going on?

June 2023Page  of 3236

And as a young adult, I had the privilege of going to London. And while there are visited the, 
the tower where the crown jewels are held. And there's a scepter there, there are all these 
crowns and just is it's the wealth, the Royal wealth of the greatest empire on the history of the 
world, are in this museum. And as the scepter that has the star of Africa. And it's just 
thousands of facets, and its on the stand that the pivots, and the bright lights, it just sparkles 
and gleams. And that its is every color imaginable. Its dynamic and fluid. It is breathtaking. You 
just gasp when you see it - just never seen anything like it. 

And I was a young man and I was thinking about getting married and had enough money to 
buy a tiny little diamond for my wife’s engagement ring. And I was looking at this and I've never 
seen anything so beautiful. This is not things that guys usually think about. And so when we, as 
an happens in all good museums in the west, you've finished a tour and there’s the gift shop. 
So I said, I'm going to buy a postcard and show my friends what a beautiful thing this diamond 
was. And I find the postcard and I get this thing, and I look at it. And I was looking with great 
excitement, but my face drops with disappointment when I look at it, because this is not what I 
saw. What I saw was something moving and ever-changing and the light was sparkling and it 
was turquoises and golds and silvers and just greens and, and violets and just all these 
different colors coming out of it. It was always moving and dynamic. And the postcard was 
fixed flat and static and cold. I thought, my friends already think I'm crazy. If I sent this picture 
saying the most beautiful thing I've ever seen, they’ll say We know he's crazy now. So I didn’t 
buy the postcard. 

On the way home God said, Do you remember your questions about Revelation four that you 
had as an eight year old? I said, yeah. He said, you spend most of your life looking at Me and 
all you’ve seen is a postcard of Me, a very poor and limited representation. But these elders, 
they spend the whole time in My presence. And every time they throw their crowns before Me 
and worship, they recover. They sit back in their 24 thrones, they lift up their eyes, and they see 
something new about Me, a new dimension of My love, a new aspect of My faithfulness, a new 
reality about My justice, or My kindness. And that revelation leads them to a new song. It's an 
ever changing song, and the word Holy holy holy really could mean unique. Awesome. One of a 
kind, spectacular.” Wow. They're just like, there is always fresh revelation. There's never been a 
song sung twice exactly the same way in His presence because this learning is of continuous 
discovery, more and more of the wonders of our God. 

And it's in anticipation of that eternal learning in His presence and that worship that creative 
dynamic, life giving worship that will be in His presence, that we develop our educational 
systems today to lead us toward that same process. And it will never be boring, never old and 
always life-giving.

Crossover Passages 
As with every sphere, the final six passages are looking at how this sphere of education 
crosses over with the other six spheres. So let's look at that together.

transcript:

Paul here is writing to one of his disciples, Titus, about false teachers that are doing harm in

title: Crossover with GOVERNMENT
transcript:
Education and Government.  
This comes from 2 Kings 12:2.  All his life Joash did what was pleasing in the Lord's sight 
because Jehoiada the priest instructed him.

This is very interesting. Joash was a king of Judah and he was actually spared. Jehoiada 
protected him because the one that before him who ruled was Athaliah, the queen, who was a 
usurper of the throne, that really came from Israel in the north. So he was protected and put on 
the throne. And as long, as long as Jehoiada was around, and when he was under the 
mentorship, the tutelage of Jehoiada things went really well. When the priest died, Joash 
ended up killing Jehoiada's son, and then he went off the rails. He went south, he turned away 
from God and all kinds of things happened and he ended up being assassinated. 

So it was a story of that was the first half of his reign was really good. And the latter part was 
very disastrous. We learned something here - the religious role can influence the other roles. 
The religious sphere can influence the government sphere or other spheres, and to bring 
educational insight into the government space. Notice that Jehoiada didn't take on a 
governmental function. As a priest he did what it was supposed to do -- to teach the ways of 
God.

So we can train people as followers of Jesus and in the spheres, in areas that we're not 
involved with as religious, Levitical or missionary people in that sphere. We can train people 
and other spheres without engaging in those spheres directly ourselves. Jehoiada remained 
faithful to in his religious role throughout his life. And it's served Joash well in his governmental 
role. 

This shows how sometimes we think we should try to keep religion out of government. We 
should keep forced religion out of government. That's the whole purpose of the separation of 
church and state that you don't impose or require or obligate people to believe in one way or 
another, because that is a personal choice. But we don't ever want to keep God out of any 
sphere. And the religious, when it’s working well can help teach government and every other 
sphere, how to bring God and his ways appropriately into that sphere. That is not manipulative. 
That is not destructive. That is not oppressive. That is life-giving and brings blessing to the 
nation.

title: Crossover with RELIGION
transcript:
Education and Religion.  
This comes from chapter eight of Nehemiah verses two to three, and then seven to eight. ...
Ezra the priest brought the Book of the Law before the assembly... (Again, probably the book 
of Deuteronomy) which included the men and women and all the children old enough to 
understand. He faced the square, just inside of the Water Gate from early morning until noon 
June 2023Page  of 3436

and read aloud to everyone who could understand. All the people listened closely to the Book 
of the Law.... The Levites... then instructed the people in the Law, while everyone remained in 
their places. They read from the Book of the Law of God and clearly explained the meaning of 
what was being read, helping the people understand each passage.

Like family and education, religion and education are to be collaborative. Here, the Levites - 
Ezra what was the priest - and the Levites, as they read the law, helped explain helped bring 
understanding. This is a context where the majority of the people would have been illiterate. 
They wouldn't have access to the, the documents themselves because those are prized 
possession with only a few copies of these scrolls. And so, as they heard, they would have 
questions and they needed to be unpacked and explained. So religion and education are to be 
rightly linked together that this Levitical role, this priestly role, this religious function can serve 
in an educational way to bless the people of the whole city or clan or tribe or nation.

title: Crossover with MEDIA/COMMUNICATION
transcript:
Education and Media Communication.  
Mark 4:33-34. with many similar parables Jesus spoke the word to them as much as they 
could understand. He did not say anything to them without using a parable. But when he was 
alone with his own disciples, he explained everything. 

Wow. He did not say anything without using a parable.

This is a literary art form. It was appropriate media communication for his day. It was

And so he used the media of his day, the means that were available to him, to educate, to

title: Crossover with CELEBRATION
transcript:
Education and Celebration.  
This is Psalm 60: the title that comes in front verse one. So in our English Bibles, there parts of 
the original text that are instructions about the psalm that we don't include in our verses, but 
they are part of the Bible. In other languages like in German, this is actually verse one.

So for the choir director: a psalm of David useful for teaching regarding that time David fought 
Aram-naharaim and Aram-zobah, and Joab returned and killed 12,000 Edomites in the Valley of 
Salt. To be sung to the tune “Lily of the Testimony.”

You know, the tune, it goes like, oh, it's okay.

The psalm was written with a didactic purpose. It was useful for teaching. It wasn't just to, for 
beauty sake, though it is aesthetically attractive, it was educationally significant. And we can 
use the arts educationally. In fact, the arts are some of the best ways to communicate the 
deepest truths. When you engage someone with a story of God, with a story of how things look 
in a sphere when its being done right. With engaging stories, where there's drama and climax 
and resolutions and redemption and conflict and all of these things, those stories and the 
visuals that either made in your imagination or on a screen or in a painting, those things all help 
us in our educational process.

We have to remember that people learn in many different ways and the arts help us advance 
the educational impact. So these two spheres should collaborate very frequently with one 
another.

Final thoughts 
As we've looked at all of this, we can see that education is connected to each of the spheres.

and pray about the sphere of education, God will lead you how to grow in this area yourself

title: God’s Ways Are Appropriate
category_title: God and the Sphere of Media/Communication
transcript:
God's Ways Are Appropriate.  
Hebrew 1:1-2. Long ago God spoke many times in many ways to our ancestors through the 
prophets. And now in these final days, he has spoken to us through his Son. God promised 
everything to the Son as an inheritance, and through the Son, he created the universe. 

God's communication is always time appropriate. We read in Isaiah 28:10, how his instructions, 
his teaching is line up online. it says he tells us everything over and over. Like, don't you love 
it? Because he, he doesn’t tire of communicating with us. I often don't get it the first time I hear 
from God or the first time I read the scriptures, I need to be taught over and over again, but he 
says one line at a time, one line at a time, a little here and a little there. He gives to us, he 
communicates with us as we are able to receive it, as it's time appropriate, and we can handle 
and we can apply and understand. 

His appropriateness is not just doing the right ‘what’ the right content or the right time. But 
even the right, ‘how’, his means of communicating. We need to pay attention to the, how he 
speaks, not just the ‘what’ he speaks. This is the same message of God's love and God's 
redemption. God's desire to forgive and reconcile human beings to himself was the same 
message all through the Original Testament and the New Testament. It's what the prophets 
spoke of. It's what the law was purposed for. It's what the scripture's all about. But even 
though that message was there and constantly present, it didn't fully land, we didn't really get 
it, until God himself came in the person of Jesus, became incarnate and lived amongst us. And 
how he communicated, the fact that he became one of us, that ‘how’ that sacrifice, that 
incredible effort to make his message understood, not just that words about his love, but a 
demonstrable practical lived out love in our midst -that ‘how’ changes us because Jesus came. 

In these final days. He has spoken to us through his son. This ‘how’ is the means of our 
salvation.

title: God’s Content Is True
category_title: God and the Sphere of Media/Communication
transcript:
God's Content Is True.  
1 Thessalonians 5:19-21, Paul writes Do not stifle the Holy Spirit. Do not scoff at prophecies, 
but test everything that is said. Hold onto what is good. 

The fact that God communicates truth is so very important. God's truth is constant and it's 
normal. We live in an age where skepticism is rampant, where everyone doubts the existence 
of objective truth. And everyone seeks their own truth. Well, whatever you feel or I feel or you 
think, or I think doesn't make - our feelings and our thoughts don't make that thing true or not 
June 2023Page  of 633

true. It's true apart from our existence, it's true because it matches reality. And God's truth, 
God's communication is always true. 

Let me just reflect some passages with you. Proverbs 30:5: Every word of God proves true... 
His commandments are right. They, when we live according to them, they prove true. Life is 
better that way. His promises are true when we trust him and he, he fulfills them. We can see 
he's trustworthy. His every word proves true. 

Isaiah 45:19 God is speaking. And he says: ... I, the Lord speak only what is true and declare 
only what is right. Are you looking for what is true and right in your life? Look to God. He's the 
only one who can guarantee that always its true, always its right. And when we think about this 
sphere, we need to model our actions after this action of God. 

Jesus, when he was before Pilate on the, at the time of just before his crucifixion said this in 
John 18:37, ...actually, I was born and came into this world to testify to the truth. All who love 
the truth recognized that what I say is true. 

When I was in university, I was in a dorm that was, you had applied to get into was kind of the, 
the college nerds, from the different fields they got together. Our dorm was different. This was 
secular university. Other dorms on weekends would have keggers and parties with beer and 
stuff. And in our dorm we would have wine and cheese parties. We were kind of very elite, very 
snobbish actually. And there were just a few of us, two or three of us who were followers of 
Jesus in that dorm. And we had all kind of different events that we ran. And so I thought at one 
point, I'm gonna do a study of 1 John with my colleagues. And so we set up five, a series of 
five days so that we can read a chapter and we've just read it and then talk about it. And the 
way I pitched it was this letter is foundational to Western civilization. So let's read about it.

So I'm having a Bible study, but it's not a Bible study, it’s a literature study because you know, 
95% of the people there are non-believers. And so I remember one day, because in 1 John it 
talks a lot about truth, one of the most brilliant guys on campus who was studying philosophy 
and you know, always on the Dean's list. I asked him and the others gathered there- Now what 
if all these comments that John is making about truth? What if they are really are objectively 
true? And this is how he answered. He said, well, I've dedicated my life to the search of truth. 
So I don't want to know if this is really true or not. Because if I find out that it's true, then my 
purpose of life is over. I won't have anything to search after. 

What? Are you kidding me? The world plays so many games with truth. They speak about 
truth, but avoid it when it brings any implications or consequences on their life. We, as 
followers of Jesus need to know there's a constant truth and we have to discover it as we 
listen to God, as we walk in his ways, we need to be a people who are consequential in 
applying that truth to our life. We don't try to shape the Bible to match our world. We need to 
shape our world to match what the Bible says. 

Finally, in the these passages you can see and Acts 14:3, Paul and Barnabas on their first 
missionary journey. They say, ...and the Lord proved their message was true by giving them 
power to do miraculous signs and wonders. And 1 Thessalonians 1:5  Paul writes: for when we 
brought you the Good News, it was not only with words, but also with power, for the Holy Spirit 
gave you full assurance that what we said was true. God’s mighty acts are confirmations. 
Those things that are absolutely impossible for the paralyzed to walk, for the dead to rise, for 
the blind to see —these are confirming signs that this is indeed the true message. 

So finally, 2 Timothy 3:14-16. Paul writes to his protege, Timothy: but you must remember...
remain faithful to the things you've been taught. You know they are true, for you know you can 
trust those who taught you. So we can know truth because it, it matches reality. You know, it's 
June 2023Page  of 733

true because it's supported by the miraculous confirmation of God. We can know it's true 
because we can see the lives of the people who've passed us on to us. You have been taught 
the holy Scriptures from childhood, and they have given you the wisdom to receive the 
salvation that comes by trusting in Christ Jesus. All scripture is inspired by God and is useful to 
teach us what is true and to make us realize what is wrong in our lives. It corrects us when we 
are wrong and teaches us to do what is right.
reflection_q1: Why is truthfulness a foundational aspect of God’s communication?

title: God’s Content Is Trustworthy
category_title: God and the Sphere of Media/Communication
transcript:
God's Content Is Trustworthy.  
Luke 1:1-4. at the outset of his gospel Luke writes these words, talking about the methodology 
he employed in order to make sure that what he was writing about we are reading is absolutely 
reliable. Many people he says, have set out to write accounts about the events that have been 
fulfilled among us. They used the eyewitness report circulating among us from the early 
disciples. Having carefully investigated everything from the beginning. I also have decided to 
write in an accurate account for you, most honorable Theopholius, so you can be certain- 
certain of the truth of everything you were taught. 

You can count on it. You can rely on it. You can build your life on it. This content is trustworthy. 
In our world there are many substantial claims that we hear about all the time. You just have to 
go online or turn on the television or listen to the radio. And, and you know, if you use this 
toothpaste you become the most popular person in your class. Those are unsubstantiated 
claims, but not so with God's word. This is not a marketing device. It's not something that's 
been invented in the boardroom, where people are trying to pitch their own products. This is 
God's word. It's reliable. It's trustworthy. You can go to the bank with it. It's absolutely true.

title: God’s Communication Is Transformative
category_title: God and the Sphere of Media/Communication
transcript:
God's Communication Is Transformative.  
Colossians 1:6 says this, This same Good News that came to you is going out all over the 
world. It is bearing fruit everywhere by changing lives, just as it changed your lives from the day 
you first heard and understood the truth about God's wonderful grace. 

God's truth is applicable. It changes lives. It was designed to reshape us, to take us wherever 
we are in our life, with whatever degree of brokenness we have and to make us into who we 
are always intended to be. Sons and daughters of the Living God. Jesus says this in John 
8:31-32 ...you are truly my disciples if you remain faithful to my teachings. And you will know 
the truth, and the truth will set you free. 

Allowing God's word to shape your life, to embrace this truth, is a freeing activity. It sets us free 
from the distortions and confusions and the habits and the addictions and the, and the issues 
that keep us bound and destroy our relationships. God's word brings transformation.

title: God’s Communication Gives Life
category_title: God and the Sphere of Media/Communication
transcript:
God's Communication Gives Life.  
John 20:30-31. The disciples saw Jesus do many other miraculous signs in addition to the 
ones recorded in this book. But these are written so that you may continue to believe that 
Jesus is the Messiah, the Son of God, and that by believing in him you will have life by the 
power of his name. 

This message, this communication, this truth of God is, is what gives us life. When we speak of 
life here, it's more than mere existence. It's more than your heart beating, and your lungs 
sucking in oxygen. It's about the fullness of life. The full realization of the potential. In John 
10:10, Jesus says my purpose is to give them a rich and satisfying life or in some translations,  
have them experience life more abundantly. We all have some measure of life, but we don't 
have it to its full. We have not realized the fullest expression imaginable. And Jesus comes to 
June 2023Page  of 833

take our life, which sometimes feels very far away from the dreams we've dreamed, the hopes 
we've yearned after. And he says, I can come and bring a true change. I can give you life that is 
complete, full, meaningful. That is sustainable. And it gives you value and dignity and worth. 

God's words are designed to do us good. You see, God is a life-Giver, He’s a life-Redeemer. 
He's a life-sustainer. He's a life-Enhancer. God is all about doing good in your life and my life. 
And if we’re to communicate like God communicates, then our communication must also give 
life, and enhance life, restore life. That's what God's purposes are for this sphere. 

SECTION INTRODUCTION 
In this next section, we're gonna look at God's purposes of communication. Why this sphere is 
so very important to our life as individuals and to our societies, to our cultures. There are very 
clear reasons why God has designed communication. You see, he created the world, made us 
in his image. He's the great communicator. He’s given us the capacity to communicate 
because he has that. Because communication is the foundation for relationship. Without 
communication you can't know one another, you can't love one another, you can't trust one 
another. So communication is foundational to the very essence of who we are as human 
beings. Beings made in the image of God, beings capable of receiving and giving love, 
responding to love- both vertically with God and horizontally with one another. Without 
communication, we can't be human. We can't be who we're intended to be. So understanding 
this is so essential. When this gets distorted in our society, human existence suffers; when this 
is done well, then human existence thrives.
reflection_q1: Why does reading God's word and the testimony of Jesus still bring abundant life today?
reflection_q2: What practical steps can communicators take to infuse their work with Jesus' life-giving message?

title: Creates Unity Around God’s Purposes
transcript:
Creates Unity Around God's Purpose.  
That's what communication is designed to do Zephaniah 3:9. Then I will purify the speech of all 
people, so that everyone can worship the Lord together. 

Our speech needs purification. Our speech is often not as it should be. It needs to be 
corrected. It needs to be adjusted. It needs to be transformed. And when it is, it allows us to 
come together. It allows for unity to happen. It creates communion and fellowship, and 
friendship. When our speech is off, relationships suffer. When our speech is on track, our 
relationships are as they should be. Contemporary media often thrives around discord. When 
there is a crisis or a problem or a conflict. Wow. The ratings go up. Everyone who wants to 
watch this thing play out and see the fight, fight, fight, fight, come. And, and they look excitedly 
at that, but that's not the way of God. Communication should not be about highlighting 
discord, but facilitating unity. This does not mean to cover up issues, but to really look at 
issues in depth so that we can address what is wrong and, and find the way to come together. 
The goal is creating a sense of common identity, of common purpose or mission in life. A 
profound harmony that supersedes cultural divisions. 

Paul writes in Romans why he wrote that letter, it wasn't primarily just to do an exposition of his 
theology. He had a pastoral purpose. He wanted to bring about change. You see, in the church, 
there were believers who were of Jewish background and others who were of Gentile 
background. And because of numerous circumstances, they were not worshiping together. 
They were each in their own separate silos. And he writes in order to help them understand 
they both have the same problem with sin. They both have the same solution to that problem, 
grace, through the made possible through Jesus sacrificial life and death on the cross of 
resurrection. And both are to come together. And so he gives the theology he gives the 
practice, then he sums up at, toward the very end of his letter this is his heart, May God who 
gives this patience and encouragement, help you live in complete harmony with each other. 

June 2023Page  of 933

That's why he writes. He wants the Jewish background believers, and Gentile background 
believers to live in complete harmony with each other as is fitting for followers of Christ Jesus. 
Then all of you can join together with one voice, giving praise and glory to God, the father of 
our Lord Jesus Christ. He is doing here exactly what Zephaniah had prophesied. He's building 
his whole theology and practice around helping them come together as one to honor God. 

lf we're communicating well, if we're communicating as we should, it should be strengthening 
unity across our societies, across our world. And having people come together, not in splitting 
them apart. When your communication, when your media is augmenting divisions, you're not 
using the media as it should. When it is helping people understand where there have been 
problems so they can address them and come together, then God's purposes are being 
fulfilled.
reflection_q1: Why is unity on of the key goals of Biblical communication?
reflection_q2: What contributes to breakdowns in unifying communication between people? How can we guard against these?
call_to_action: What proactive measure are you inspired to take today?

title: Creates Life-Enhancing Situations
transcript:
Creates Life-Enhancing Situations.  
Proverbs 10:11. The words of the godly are a life giving fountain. The words of the wicked 
conceal violent intentions. 

As I was getting ready to film this set, I went to the barbershop yesterday, cut my hair. And I 
walked in and I had a, a lady come and take me through the chair to cut my hair. And she was 
maybe in little late forties, early fifties. And I asked, how are you doing? She kind of grunted 
said, are you doing okay? And she, oh, it's just the work. I said, having a bad day? Oh, you 
know, it's just the work. And you could tell she was, she was doing what she was doing, you 
know, because she had to get the salary and pay the bills and take care of her family and do 
the things that people do. And so many people do the work that it’s because they have to, 
there's not finding joy fulfillment in it. 

So I sat there and she started cutting my hair, and I struck a up a conversation with her. I said, I 
said, you know, you do something really important. I said, you know, when people come in 
here or they come in because they don't like the way they look. Their, their hair doesn't look 
good. And, and by the time you finished in the half hour, you show them the mirror at the end 
and go - Wow, I look good. I'm looking great. And you, you, for what you do, you give them a 
sense of renew sense, worth of value of, of affirmation. And she looked at me said, wow, you 
know, I haven't thought of that. And I just held up a mirror to her. She would hold up a mirror 
for me to see how my hair was cut. I held up a mirror to her so you could see her, her life was 
touching people. 

She'd been doing this for about 30 years. She said asking, do you know how many people you 
would've touched in those times? The thousands of not thousands. And her eyes grew big. 
And my communication just enabled her to see that her life mattered. But she was doing more 
than just getting a paycheck. She was more than just meeting the needs of her family. She was 
doing good in her community and her society. 

God's words enhance life. And we can do this anywhere, everywhere. It doesn't have to be 
when you just give a religious message, you can just speak truth and affirmation. Proverbs 
18:21 says the tongue can bring death or life. Words are powerful. Use them wisely. You can 
use the words to enhance life or to tear it down. Jesus said in John 6:63, the very words I have 
spoken to you are spirit and life. He comes to enhance our life. Peter, a few verses later 
responds and affirms that what Jesus said is actually true. He said, you have the words that 
give eternal life. John 6:68, Jesus has words that give life eternal life. And if we model 
ourselves after him, our words can be life givers too. 

June 2023Page  of 1033
reflection_q1: Why does life-enhancing communication matter in today’s media landscape?
reflection_q2: How can careful words bring renewed perspective or hope to someone in hardship?

title: Creates Community and Cultural Uniqueness
transcript:
Creates Community and Cultural 
Uniqueness.  
This comes from Esther 8:9. So on June 25, the king's secretaries were summoned, and a 
decree was written exactly as Mordecai dictated. It was sent to the Jews and to the highest 
officers, the governors and the nobles of all the 127 provinces stretching from India to Ethiopia. 
The decree was written in the scripts and languages of all the peoples of the empire, including 
that of the Jews. 

This was a massive communication effort, done by the government to all these distant 
geographies, to ethno-linguistic diversity with all these languages. And this communication led 
to action. It allowed the Jews to defend themselves against those who had sought to wipe 
them out through a total genocide. And this was a, this communication changed the history for 
this one people. This communication was later immortalized in a common festival. Purim is an 
opportunity to celebrate God's miraculous intervention, to save the Jews who are about to be 
exterminated. 

But how did that salvation happen? it happened through the means of the timely and 
appropriate communication that spoke to their community and gave them a unique response, a 
common language and a common communication creates a sense of common identity. We are 
on the same page with one another. We need a common story. We need in every people group, 
in every nation, every family, every tribe, what is this God storyline? 

We all have brokenness in our storylines, but we can also all have redemption. And God by His 
Mercy has intervened. And that storyline that we can pass on from one generation to another, 
as the Jewish people do year after year, when they celebrate Purim and remember how 
thousands of years ago God saved them from the hands of the Persian oppressors and how 
He intervened. That story gives identity. It gives a sense of purpose and it passes on values 
and fosters multi-generational continuity. This is what communication does. Good 
communication.

title: Provides Trustworthy Information
transcript:
Provides Trustworthy Information.  
2 Timothy 2:2. You have heard me teach things that have been confirmed by many reliable 
witnesses. Now teach them these truths to other trustworthy people who be able to pass them 
on to others. 

So Paul taught Timothy was to teach somebody else who then pass on and teach another —
that’s four generations of passing on communication. One of the beauties of the scriptural 
record is how very, very honest it is. It's radically honest. It's unlike any other cultural writing 
because, in most stories, the key players are, tend to be idealized and turned into super 
heroes. But the key players in the Bible are not fictional characters. They're not these great 
men and women who always do things right. They've not been made into super heroes. We, 
we read about them with all their warts and failures and foibles and concerns and fears. We 
read of Abraham and how he lacked courage. And Jacob's deceitfulness, of Moses's 
disobedience, of David's moral failure, of Peter's betrayal. And Thomas' doubting, of Paul's 
murderous past. And yet God worked through these imperfect people. 

When you read these stories, you can get the feel, no one's trying to cover up their pasts. 
These are, these are authentic, real people. They have problems. They have failures like you 
and I do, perhaps even more so. And so when we read their story to go like, oh, God worked in 
their lives. This gives us great hope and confidence. He can work in my life, in your life as well. 

June 2023Page  of 1133

You know, the greatest story in the Bible is the resurrection. And when the gospel stories first 
write about it, in Mark it says the women came and told them. And you know, in the Roman 
and the Greek and the Jewish cultures, women were not allowed as witnesses in court. They 
were considered unreliable. If you want to prove your case, make it rock solid in that culture, 
whether you're talking to Greek speaking or Roman citizens or Jewish background people, 
believers, you don't say, well, we're building this on the testimony of women. It was like in that 
cultural context, it was like, Uhuh, this is not gonna be any good. 

And then this says, the disciples did not believe. And so that after they saw Jesus, they did not 
believe it was him. Like, if you're trying to be like, Hey, we are so amazing. We're like the great 
apostles, we saw Jesus. We knew it right from the start. He was gonna rise from the dead. No, 
they have Jesus face to face and they go, mm, I don't know. 

So when they actually are changed and understand through the Holy Spirit, all that God has 
done. Its like, this is not a put on. This is not fictional. And you don't make these things up. 
They the, if you are making it up, you do it all wrong. It's not a Hollywood movies where the 
superheros can jump out of airplanes without a parachute and land and not have a scratch on 
his face. It's just like, hello. These are not fabricated tales, pitching a false narrative. They are 
ruthlessly honest account that are, therefore, abundantly trustworthy. The Bible, God's word, is 
true to fact. It matches reality. We can be confident of it and pass it on the next generation and 
encourage them to keep doing the same thing, because it is trustworthy information.
reflection_q1: Why is the trustworthiness of information crucial in communication and educational contexts?
reflection_q2: What can leaders do to intentionally pass on truth to the next generation and what pitfalls should they avoid?

title: Provides Transformative Information
transcript:
Provides Transformative Information.  
Isaiah 61:1-2. These are the first words that Jesus reads in the gospel of Luke when he is in 
the, in the synagogue of Nazareth, he says: The Spirit of the Sovereign Lord is upon me, for the 
Lord has anointed me to bring good news to the poor. He has sent me to comfort the 
brokenhearted and to proclaim that captives will be released and prisoners will be freed. He 
has sent me to tell those who mourn that the time of the Lord's favor has come, and with it, the 
day of the Lord's anger against their enemies. 

God's Spirit led communication should always bring comfort, hope, freedom, healing, 
restoration, rejoicing. These are the things that are described in these words of Isaiah, which 
Jesus quoted that were the fruit of his ministry, of his communication. As we learned to 
communicate like he did, they should be the fruit, the transformative impact of our 
communication as well. See, the story of the gospel is simple. It's this that God is with us. God 
is for us. And he invites us into relationship with himself and that relational truth re- shapes our 
life completely if we will allow it. That's God's communication made true, made real, made 
practical in Jesus, and still alive, still bringing transformation to this very day. 

SECTION INTRODUCTION 
The next 11 passages all have to do with communication that we should seek to imitate. The 
Bible gives us so many examples of how to communicate well. So let's look at these together.

title: Inspired by the Spirit
transcript:
Inspired By The Spirit.  
Matthew 22:43, Jesus responded, Then why does David, speaking under the inspiration of the 
Spirit, call Messiah My Lord... 

Jesus knows that David was not speaking just his own thoughts, but he was inspired by the 
Spirit. This is something we should all seek to do in our communication. We should seek to 
align our words with that of the thoughts and the words, the communication of the Spirit of 
God. And what this requires of us of course, is that we, before we speak, we need to listen. We 
June 2023Page  of 1233

need to listen to God. We need to reflect on his character. We need to reflect on his purposes. 
We need to meditate in his presence and understand what he's trying to say to us. And 
therefore what he’d like to communicate through us. And it's this place of intimacy of pursuit of 
God, of seeking to align ourselves with his Spirit that gives strength and power and anointing 
authority to our communication. So let's seek to the Spirit of God, allow him to inspire us to 
breathe his life and his words into us. As we seek to follow him.

title: Speak like Jesus
transcript:
Speak Like Jesus.  
Matthew 7:28-29. When Jesus had finished saying these things, the crowds were amazed at 
his teaching, for he taught with real authority - quite unlike their teachers in religious law. 

You know, if you go through this sermon on the mount, Matthew 5,6, and 7, there's hardly 
anything new or novel in Jesus’s teaching. If you look at the Rabbinic teaching those 
instructions, those records that are the Jewish faith outside of the Bible. Almost everything that 
Jesus said had been said by a rabbi previous to Jesus- turn the other cheek, love your enemy. 
Those were not - Jesus was not the first one to say. That because there are logical, natural 
conclusions or implications of reflection and meditation on the scriptures of the Original 
Testament. 

So what he said wasn't so extraordinary. It's how he said it. It's not because he had a very nice 
voice and delivered it well. It was not about his methodology. It's that his life supported his 
words. His words were an echo of his life. He had lived what he was teaching. And so there 
was a resonance. There was an authenticity, there was a genuineness about his words that 
otherwise would not be heard in the words of someone who said the right things, but lived the 
wrong way. 

Jesus lived the right way and said the right things. And so when he spoke, people felt how 
genuine, real, authentic, powerful, and anointed his words were, because they're backed up by 
a life well- lived. If we want to be great communicators, we need to learn how to live life well. 
We need to live life obediently. We need to apply the truths of God to us first, and then when 
we speak to others, they, our words will carry that much more weight.
reflection_q1: Why is it important to model our communication on Jesus’ approach?
reflection_q2: What strategies can be implemented in modern media to incorporate Jesus' communication principles effectively?

title: Align Your Communication with God’s
transcript:
Align Your Communication With God’s.  
Revelation 22:17 says this the Spirit and the bride say, “Come.” Let anyone who hears this say, 
“Come,”... 

This is a wonderful passage. It's almost a very last verse, getting close to the end of the Bible. 
And here we see the people of God, their words match the words of God. They, the Spirit says 
come. The brides says come. Everyone who listens should say come. This is absolutely 
marvelous. This is what we should do. And it's only possible as we listen and imitate what we 
hear. Well, that's, I want to do something new and novel and different you. Yes, but you don't 
wanna do anything new and novel that's different from God. A child listens to the father and 
mothers is learning to speak it. Everything that the father and mother say, the child repeats as 
closely has the child can. And that's how you learn to communicate well. It’s through the 
imitation of the parent, the one who gives you life. And God is the one who gives life to us all. 
So we must listen to him. 

The Latin word from which we get the word ‘confess' in English literally means to say the same 
thing as. When we confess, we are agreeing with or saying the same thing as another person. 
Usually we think of confessing as associated with our sin, that when we have done something 
wrong, we acknowledge that we've done something wrong. When we confess. What we're 
June 2023Page  of 1333

actually doing is we're saying when God brings conviction, we're acknowledging our guilt. And 
we are lining our perspective with God's perspective. 

You know, oftentimes if I might be having a, a devotional time with the Lord and God reminds 
me of a conversation I had the other day, and he says, David didn't know that conversation 
with this friend. Yes. He said, you lied. And I think back and oh, no, God, I didn't lie. I wasn't 
totally accurate, God. No, David, you lied. Well, I, I, I twisted the truth maybe a little bit. I 
exaggerated God, no, David, you, you lied. And see, as long as my words don't match his 
words, confession hasn't happened. But when I say, yes God, you're right. I acknowledge that I 
have lied. And my words matches and I've confessed. And that usually then leads to 
repentance and change of life in that example.

But we can confess other things when God says David you're loved and I'm feeling kind of 
down and discouraged. I go, oh nah, I'm just, I'm no good. David, you're loved and you're 
precious. And whatever God is saying to you, to me, we need to align our communication with 
his because he speaks truth. We often do not. And the more we align our communication, we 
confess, we listen carefully to what he's saying to us and make our words like his. Then we 
learn to communicate in a way that gives life to us and others who will hear us.
reflection_q1: Why is it important to first align our thoughts with God's, to be effective communicators?
reflection_q2: What would it look like for media professionals to align their communication with God's by listening, imitating, and confessing in agreement with His perspective?

title: Speak the Truth in Love
transcript:
Speak the Truth in Love.  
Ephesians 4:15. Instead we will speak the truth in love growing in every way more and more 
like Christ, who is the head of his body, the church. 

This is a wonderful passage. It's very easy to read and it sure is challenging to live out. Literally  
this passage doesn't have the word speak, the communication is understood. But in the Greek 
if we translated it literally it would be,  we will, we will truth in love or truthing in love that we 
need to communicate in such a way. That truth and love are wed together in an inseparable 
way. 

You know, truth without love is not genuine, true, authentic truth. And love without truth is not 
genuine, true, authentic love. Truth and love need one another. Truth and love both exists in the 
person of God. They're both attributes of God. God is a truth teller. God is a lover and he 
doesn't sacrifice one to do the other. And if you think sometimes, well I just, you get irritated 
with a person and you're upset you say, I just need to tell him the truth, to tell it like it is. And 
you, you just abandon any thought of love or kindness or graciousness. And you just do that ... 
Boy, that tends to mess up the relationship, doesn’t it? I know that probably we can all 
remember times where we've said things that we shouldn’t have said. But it's we need to keep 
these two blended together. They are inseparable in God and they need to become inseparable 
in us. Indivisible. Totally linked, totally hooked together. See truth is not true if it's not spoken in 
the Spirit of truth. And the Spirit of truth is always kind and loving and gracious. Even when 
correction is needed. Even when exhortation is required. It's always, always done in a loving 
way. When it's done, according to the Spirit of the truth.
reflection_q1: Why is truth better received when it is spoken in love?
reflection_q2: What outcomes can be expected when media professionals commit to reporting and storytelling with truth in love?

title: Speak Seasoned with Grace
transcript:
Speak Seasoned with Grace.  
Colossians 4:6. Let your conversation be gracious and attractive so that you will have a right 
response for everyone. 

Seasoned. So when you take your food, the main staple and you sprinkle salt or coriander or 
cumin, or peppers or chilis or, oregano, something on it- and you take what is ordinary food 
and you spice it up. It becomes beautiful. It becomes memorable. It becomes wonderful. It 
seasoned just a little bit, but it makes all the difference to the flavors of the food and the 
experience of your meal time. And that's what grace does in our communication. You don't 
June 2023Page  of 1433

have to pile it on, but it is just enough to flavor everything that you say, everything you 
communicate. You see grace is what gives hope to the hopeless. It gives a way out to people 
who are feeling trapped, it brings confidence to those who are distraught. It clarifies the 
confused and encourages the discouraged. It forgives those who have failed and extends 
friendship to those who feel unlovable and who are feeling unloved. Grace changes all of that. 
And if we communicate well, if we communicate as God intends us to communicate, our 
words, seasoned with grace, bring transforming life-giving change to our hearers.
reflection_q1: Why does grace act as a bridge in good communication?
reflection_q2: What are practical ways media profesionals can comunicate "seasoned with grace" while still addressing problems?

title: Speak in Order to Build Up
transcript:
Speak in Order to Build Up.  
1 Corinthians 14:26. Well, my brothers and sisters, lets summarize. When you meet together, 
one will sing, another will teach, another will tell some special revelation God has given, one 
will speak in tongues, and another will interpret what is said. But everything that is done must 
strengthen all of you. 

Everything that is done must strengthen all of you. 

Good communication fosters growth and development, pointing people in the right direction, 
championing and affirming their potential, helping them find their way forward. Good 
communication does not tear people down. It is not snide, it is never a cynical. It does not 
leave the listener stuck in their current life. It helps them see -there’s a solution. There’s a hope, 
there's a way out. It helps them grow. It helps them develop. It's always serving them. This is 
what you need to think of - is my communication serving somebody else? Paul says everything 
that is done must strengthen all of you. Are you strengthening people? Are you helping them? 
Are you assisting them? Are you empowering them? That's what good communication does.

reflection_q2: What impact could it have on society when the media industry focuses on building up individuals and communities?

title: Speak in Order to Encourage
transcript:
Speak in Order to Encourage.  
Acts 9:27. Then Barnabas brought him to the apostles and told him how Saul had seen the

had preached boldly in the name of Jesus in Damascus. 

We were first introduced to Barnabas in Acts 4:36. And we find out that his name isn't actually 
Barnabas. It’s Joseph. Barnabas is his nickname, that literally means ‘son of encouragement’. 
Because Barnabas lived as a door opener. He put his own life on the line and he took the risk 
to communicate on behalf of others who were not trusted or respected by the majority of his 
peers. The first time we see this happen is with Peter's case. You can imagine the startup of 
the church numbers are growing. He's a fisherman. He's never handled this logistics of a 
operation this size. He’s a new leader in unfamiliar circumstances and there are lots of needs, 
lots of needs and lots of reasons why he should be discouraged and, and pushed to the side. 

And Barnabas has like all the credentials that Peter doesn't have. Peter doesn't have a 
religious background. Barnabas is Levite, he’s been trained. He's comes from a very wealthy 
family. He's he is got the social and the economic and religious credentials. And he doesn't 
say, Peter, move over - I can do this job better than you. He goes and sells his own property 
and puts all the money to support Peter. This action is one of the best communication 
exercises in the Bible. Actions often speak louder than words and because he, he came and 
supported the leadership instead of challenging them saying, Hey, you guys don't understand 
how to do this. Stand aside, I'll take over. He cast his lot in with them. He supported them. That 
was huge. 

What if Barnabas had not done that at that moment? But Peter had just come out of jail and 
had just, it was a lot of questioning of what was going on in Jerusalem. He was probably very 
insecure at that point. And Barnabas helped him become the leader that he came to be.

We see later that Barnabas goes and finds Saul, when everyone had been afraid of Saul 
because he'd been a persecutor of the church and, and he brings him to Damascus and uh, 
and he, he brings him to the leaders of the church in Jerusalem. And he speaks to them about 
how this man is trustworthy. You can imagine they would've been afraid - Do we trust this guy? 
Is he just feigning conversion so he can get into our inner circle and then take us all captive 
and kill us like he has killed others in the previous life? And Barnabas risks his reputation and

And now just think about this. These are become the two most prominent leaders of the New 
Testament church, Peter and Paul, and both of them in a moment when the church might have

encouraged them in very concrete ways, not just nice words, but costly things - put his life on

So he was there at decisive moments in the leadership development. 

So then think about this. Peter is either directly or indirectly responsible for three books in the

And Paul is responsible for 15 books directly indirectly. So the 13 letters he writes plus Luke

title: Speak with Self-control
transcript:
Speak with Self Control.  
I'm gonna read from James 3:2-12. This is a longer passage, but boy, do we need to read it - 
Do I need to read this again, and again and again. Let's listen to the word of God. Indeed, we 
all make many mistakes. For if we could control our tongues, we would be perfect and we

We can make a large horse go wherever we want by means of a small bit in its mouth. And a 
small rudder makes a huge ship turn wherever the pilot chooses to go, even though the winds 
are strong. In the same way, the tongue is a small thing that makes grand speeches. 

But a tiny spark is set a great forest on fire. And among all the parts of the body, the tongue is 
a flame of fire. It is a whole world of wickedness, corrupting your entire body. It can set your 
whole life on fire, for it is set on fire by hell itself. 

People can tame all kinds of animals, birds, reptiles, and fish, but no one can tame the tongue.

cursing come pouring out of the same mouth. Surely, my brother and sisters, this is not right! 
Does a spring of water bubble out with both fresh water and bitter water? Does a fig tree 
produce olives, or a grapevine produce figs? No, and you can't draw fresh water from the salty 
spring.

wish we could have taken those words and put them back in our mouth and just eaten them, 
just bitten our tongue just a second longer, because relationships have been broken. Pain has 
been compounded and all kinds of complications have arisen, because of things that you've 
said, because of things I've said. Tongue control is of paramount importance. This area of all 
the areas of self control, what he’s saying it's not that the tongue is more, the physical part of

with our tongue. 

Just saying an unkind word, a word that denigrates or demeans or yeah, just the cynical or 
robs someone of hope or of courage. All of us can do this. 

So tongue control is of paramount importance, not just in our personal relationships, but in the 
public media. Let's consider how is our media? Is everything that being said, stuff that's on the 
internet, online, in the news media, in print, on radio - Is it giving life? We should not print, 
broadcast, tweet, post, everything that comes to mind. Just like you should not say everything 
to your friend that comes to mind. Exercise self control, tongue control, communication 
control. Run your communication through a grid. We have just looked at a variety of things we 
need to imitate. We looked at it is our communication truthful? Is it loving? Is it graceful? Does 
it build up? Does it encourage? If the communication doesn't do these things, don't do it. 
Don't tweet it. Don’t post it. Don't go through with it. Bite your tongue. Sometimes I feel like I 
got teeth marks on my tongue from just holding things back. But if it doesn't be pass the test 
of loving, truthful, gracious, building up encouraging, keep it to yourself. 

Authenticity is not a license for lack of self control. Discernment is a true mark of maturity. And 
knowing when to just be quiet is a mark of trusting God who can do things greater than your 
wrong communication will ever do.

title: Listen to Godly Advice
transcript:
Listen to Godly Advice.  
Proverbs 25:11 says this: Timely advice is lovely, like golden apples in a silver basket. 

Listen, communication is a two-way street. It’s not just about what you say, but how you listen. 
It always begins by listening. It goes beyond hearing. You can hear something and not really be 
paying attention to it. You know, listening means you hear and you are focused. You're 
thoughtful, you're intentional, you're purposeful. And it doesn't go in one ear and out the other, 
it stays in there. That's listening. The Hebrew is Shema and the word for Shama both to listen 
and to obey. It’s really the old English word to “heed” - to pay attention in order to apply. And 
so we need to listen to godly advice. That's where communication always begins. 

You know, I often think God has a sense of humor. Just look at someone else's face. Look at 
mine. I've two ears and only one mouth. I think it's like kind of like a hint -Listen twice, speak 
once. And it's we need to give a lot more attention to listening. Well today we have so much 
power at our fingertips. We can communicate with the whole world. We can press a few 
buttons and millions of people can listen to us or watch us. And yet, if we have not listened 
well, our, our words could be destructive. James 1:19 says you must all be quick to listen, slow 
to speak and slow to get angry. Quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to get angry. 

June 2023Page  of 1733
reflection_q1: Why is learning to listen well so important in wise communication?
reflection_q2: What societal changes can be achieved when media content is influenced by godly advice and wisdom?
